note_data.title: Current electricity
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated using ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object moving in a circular path at constant speed has constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, therefore it must be accelerating
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to the motion of the object towards the centre of the circle
Centripetal acceleration is given by a = v²/r = ω²r, where v is linear speed, r is radius, and ω is angular speed
Centripetal force is calculated using F = mv²/r = mω²r, derived from Newton's second law F = ma
A gravitational field is an area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
Newton's law of gravitation states that gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
Gravitational field strength in a radial field follows g = GM/r², showing an inverse square relationship with distance

definitions.definition: The angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration experienced by an object moving in a circular path due to constantly changing velocity direction
The force that acts perpendicular to the motion of an object moving in a circle, always directed towards the centre
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly with correct formula, substitution and unit

modelAnswer.question: A battery has EMF 12.0 V and internal resistance 2.0 Ω. Calculate the terminal potential difference when a current of 3.0 A flows. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer:
Terminal p.d. = EMF - (current × internal resistance)
Terminal p.d. = 12.0 - (3.0 × 2.0)
Terminal p.d. = 12.0 - 6.0 = 6.0 V
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formula V = E - Ir
Mark 3: Correct final answer with unit

commonMistakes: Confusing linear speed with angular speed - remember v = ωr relates them
Thinking that constant speed in circular motion means no acceleration - velocity changes due to changing direction
Forgetting that centripetal force acts towards the centre, not outwards
Not recognizing that gravitational field strength follows an inverse square relationship with distance
Assuming gravitational force is measured from surface rather than centre of masses
examinerTips: Remember to convert between degrees and radians using the factors π/180 and 180/π respectively
Always check whether you need centripetal force or acceleration - use F = ma to convert between them
For inverse square relationships, if distance doubles, the field strength becomes one quarter of the original value
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Circuit diagram showing battery with EMF E and internal resistance r connected to external resistance R, with voltmeter across external resistance and ammeter in series
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate current, voltage and resistance in a series circuit with three resistors and explain why current is the same throughout
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) tested understanding of ammeter placement in circuits — many students incorrectly drew it in parallel
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) required candidates to explain why connecting batteries in parallel increases current capacity but not voltage
N/D 2023 P2 Q6 (7 marks) combined current electricity with heating effect — students had to calculate power dissipation and explain energy transformations
M/J 2021 P1 Q18 (1 mark) tested the relationship between charge, current and time using Q = It

workedExample.question: A 12V battery is connected to two resistors: 4Ω and 6Ω in series. Calculate (a) the total resistance (b) the current in the circuit (c) the voltage across each resistor. [6 marks]
steps: Step 1: Calculate total resistance in series: Rtotal = R1 + R2 = 4Ω + 6Ω = 10Ω
Step 2: Use Ohm's law to find current: I = V/R = 12V ÷ 10Ω = 1.2A
Step 3: Calculate voltage across 4Ω resistor: V1 = I × R1 = 1.2A × 4Ω = 4.8V
Step 4: Calculate voltage across 6Ω resistor: V2 = I × R2 = 1.2A × 6Ω = 7.2V
workedExample.answer: (a) Total resistance = 10Ω (b) Current = 1.2A (c) Voltage across 4Ω = 4.8V, voltage across 6Ω = 7.2V
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct resistance formula and calculation, 2 marks for correct current calculation showing working, 2 marks for both voltage calculations, 1 mark for appropriate units throughout

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused series and parallel rules' — always remember that current is the same in series circuits but voltage divides, while voltage is the same in parallel circuits but current divides
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently drew ammeters in parallel instead of series' — ammeters must always be connected in series to measure current flowing through a component
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often forgot to include units in their final answers' — always include appropriate units (A for current, V for voltage, Ω for resistance) to avoid losing marks
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw circuit diagrams showing: battery symbol (long and short parallel lines), resistor symbol (rectangular box), ammeter symbol (circle with 'A'), voltmeter symbol (circle with 'V'). For series circuits, show all components in a single loop with ammeter in series. For parallel circuits, show components on separate branches with voltmeter connected across components being measured. Include proper connection points and ensure current flow direction is consistent.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular quantities measured in radians, with angular speed related to linear speed by ω = v/r. Objects in circular motion experience centripetal acceleration and force directed towards the centre. Gravitational fields cause attractive forces between masses that follow an inverse square law with distance. Field strength decreases rapidly as distance from the source increases.
